title: Feed write throughput is bounded by neighborhood propagation, not upload bandwidth
content:
## Claim

The throughput ceiling on a single feed is **network propagation of the SOC through the responsible neighborhood**, not the bandwidth between the client and its Bee node.

## Evidence

- Bee processes feed writes sequentially per topic because SOC index is monotonic
- A newly-written SOC at index N must be stored by the responsible neighborhood before a reader at any other node will see it — and therefore before index N+1's causal "based on" is reliable
- The adapter's 3-second debounce already batches rapid edits into single writes, so per-topic write rate is typically well below the propagation limit
- The throttled flush (max 5 concurrent writes) prevents overloading the node across topics
- Quoted key insight from source: *"the bottleneck is not upload speed but feed propagation time (SOC must be stored by the neighborhood before the next write is visible)."*

## Why it matters

Tuning the debounce window smaller than propagation time buys nothing — you just queue writes behind each other. It reframes performance work: invest in propagation-side strategies (mutable stamps, GSOC notifications, advance retrieve requests) rather than client upload parallelism for single-feed scenarios.
description: A SOC must be stored by the responsible neighborhood before the next index is visible, so feed write rate is propagation-limited even on a fast local node.
